title: This YouTube Change Affects Every Single Video You Upload

transcript.text: [laughter] >> I am really crushing this content creator life today. >> So this is an AI-generated version of me that YouTube can legally make. And they can do the same thing to you because you've already said yes. But before you rage quit this entire endeavor, I do have some good news. Yeah, you can pretty much shut all of this down if you know where to look. By the end of this video, I'll show you how. But first of all, we need to know why all of this is happening. Why copyright law doesn't protect you in this case, [music] which you might think it should do, and what's it got to do with all of this? I think it's time we called in an expert. >> I would like to ask your your viewers and listeners how many of them have ever read the terms and conditions on YouTube. >> This is Paul Quinn, and he's been a copyright lawyer for almost 30 years. And he's asking a very simple, yet dangerous, question. So let us know in the comments below with a simple yes or no, have you ever read YouTube's terms of service? When I asked this question on our community tab, this was the rather embarrassing result. And I'll be honest with you, if it wasn't part of my job, I wouldn't read them either. And even though I have read them, I barely understand them. But it is really important because on day one of opening a YouTube account, you're entering into a contract. >> All contracts that you sign um in any aspect of life are based on leverage. >> And when it comes to that so-called leverage, it's very much weighted in YouTube's favor. >> For example, let's say you wanted to open your own YouTube channel. You can't write to YouTube and say, "Send me your terms and conditions and I will redline them." >> Yeah, you can't pick and choose the terms to agree to. You're either all in or you're all out. The classic term of service to point to is this one. You grant to YouTube the right to monetize your content on the service. The agreement does not entitle you to any payments. So unfortunately, for all those creators who are complaining about ads running on their videos before they've been monetized or even worse, ads running on their videos after they've been demonetized, that is a term of service you agreed to. But, what about copyright? Let's let Paul define it first in its most basic terms. >> When you create something that is original uh and it is written down, recorded, something you can hold and or see, you automatically are the copyright holder for that piece. When you are the copyright holder, there are certain rights that belong to you and you alone. And that is the right to publish it, the right to distribute it, the right to copy it, um the right to make what's called a derivative work, meaning something that's very similar to it or based upon it. >> And this is what happens when you upload the thing you created to YouTube. >> When you upload something to a social media platform, you are giving that social media platform the right to do all those things. So, they can distribute it. They can copy it. Um uh and they can In some instances, they can create derivative works as well. >> Now, that last one, derivative works, has become a very interesting one recently with the release of YouTube's Gemini Omni feature. It essentially allows any YouTube creator to take another YouTube creator short and prompt it into a new piece of content. And as a result, random people are taking this YouTube short of me and prompting it into an AI version of me doing stuff like this. >> This is the best moment ever. Let us celebrate with a THE DANCE. >> AND you might be thinking at this point, "Hang on, are people allowed to do this without my permission?" To which the answer is, "Yes, it's in YouTube's terms of service." You also grant each other user of the service a worldwide, non-exclusive, royalty-free license to access your content through the service and to use that content only as enabled by a feature of the service. Yep, that terms of service that's been there for years fits the criteria of this new feature that's been out only a couple of weeks. So, the summary here is that you do still own the original video that you created, but you have licensed it to YouTube to do with as they please. And it may well be argued that this is merely the cost of business of working with YouTube. You can upload as much content as you want to YouTube and they will promote that content to billions of users. And that does sound like a fair trade. Except for the fact that you didn't realize it was a trade because 83% of you never read any of this. But all of this leads to the next question. Now that you know all of this, will it make any difference? Are you going to stop creating content and close down your YouTube account because the terms of service give the platform too much leverage? My guess is every single one of you is going to say no. The YouTube adventure is far too exciting and there's so much opportunity on the platform. So, while it may be true that 83% of you have never read any of this YouTube gobbins, 100% of you simply don't care. Now, one thing that does remain true about copyright is that other creators simply can't rip your content off of YouTube and then re-upload it themselves without it at least appearing in your content ID screen. And from here, you can decide whether or not to exercise your right as a copyright holder to take action such as a copyright strike and taking down the footage. But what about all of this AI-generated content? Whether or not it's a version of me or someone else, where, right now, does copyright stand on all of this? >> What they have said, their guidance as of today, is that anything created with AI cannot be copyrighted. >> Now, the "they" in this instance are the US Copyright Office who basically said a clever prompt doesn't make you the author. But of course, when it comes to AI, it's not as simple as that. Unless there is >> substantial human involvement. And with all due respect to the copyright office, what on earth does that mean? >> Mhm, this question is becoming a bit of a recurring theme when it comes to YouTube and demonetization, isn't it? No doubt that question has been asked a thousand times over when it comes to inauthentic content. What on earth does that mean? But back to AI-generated content and copyright, fortunately Paul can at least present us with an example. >> You can copyright the non-AI parts. So let's say you use AI to write the lyrics for a song that you have written the melody and the parts for, then you can still copyright the melody and the parts, but the lyrics are not subject to copyright. And don't forget what that means is if somebody else likes those lyrics, they can just use them for their material free and clear. >> So to be clear on what's being said here, which I hasten to add should not be taken as legal advice. If you ask your favorite AI chatbot to write, create, or generate something 100% by itself, then that is not copyrighted content. But if you add something to that prompt which you created or you refine the output that was received from that chatbot, then it does get a little bit more complicated. Now as for the remixing of my likeness or anybody else's likeness, well, surprisingly that's an entirely different thing. >> The use of AI to use someone's you know, pictures, faces, that is not actually subject to copyright, but it is subject to trademark. So you would be able to file a trademark for your likeness and voice because they are unique to you and you use them in commerce. >> But, there is a cost attached to that that runs into the hundreds, if not thousands of dollars, which does seem a little unfair to stop other people creating AI-generated content of yourself. Fortunately, there is a built-in fix for this inside the YouTube studio. If you go into the video details of a short, you can turn off video remixing, which will solve the problem. At least for now, at least on YouTube. I can't speak for the other platforms and their intentions. And that's simply down to the reality that the platforms are going to protect themselves first because they have legislation on their side to do that. >> So, the Digital Millennium Copyright Act is an act that was designed to protect social media companies from claims of copyright dispute. >> And that is essentially the whole engine. The platform is legally protected as long as it pulls flagged content fast, and it's never obliged to back you in these circumstances, which means that in reality >> you can't ever rely on YouTube, Facebook, TikTok, social media to do the right thing, right? But, just cuz it's impossible. >> And just to give you an idea of what happens if copyright arguments leave YouTube and end up in court. >> The average amount of time it takes a copyright case to get through federal court, it takes nearly 2 years, and the average cost um to prosecute a copyright case is about $150,000. >> So, you're probably too small to get sued, right? But, a copyright strike doesn't need a courtroom and 150 grand. A copyright complaint often means that the platform is covering its own butt. And this is happening so fast that the laws underpinning all of this are still being written. >> There is no doubt that the law has not yet caught up. The law is always slow. It tends to be reactionary. Um and and so, that's not terribly helpful. And there's no clearer area that that's the case than in the field of of AI. >> And he's not wrong. Last year, in a landmark AI case, two courts ruled within days of each other that training AI on copyrighted books was fair use. However, at the same time, both court cases still warned that AI-generated outputs could infringe on copyright. It all sounds like at this early stage, even the courts are guessing on this topic. There are three key learnings to take out of this video. When you upload a video to YouTube, you're basically handing over your copyright of that content along with a bunch of other things that at least 83% of you didn't have a clue about. And as for content that is 100% AI-generated, according to the somewhat fuzzy copyright law, it can't be copyrighted. And YouTube, along with every other social media platform, is going to protect itself first from copyright before it considers your position. And I guess the bonus key learning here is that if you're going to the YouTube studio and set your video to audio remixing only, then you don't have to worry about any of this AI cloning of your own likeness, at least for now. But, I did briefly mention inauthentic content, didn't I? And certainly in the last 12 months, AI has certainly tested what that is, how it's applied, and what you must do if you get demonetized through inauthentic content. And naturally, I've made a video all about it over here if you want to learn more. I'll see you in there. Let's see if we can time our clicks at the same time. 3 2 1.
